executive_summary.one_paragraph: CD81 is a tetraspanin (4-pass TM) with high, broadly validated plasma-membrane surface accessibility across diverse human primary and cell-line contexts. Multiple independent direct methods — live-cell flow cytometry (AML blasts, HEK293T, retinal organoids, primary monocytes/T cells), surface biotinylation/LC-MS/MS (fibroblasts, glioma lines, PBMCs), and immunogold EM on EVs — converge on an unambiguous surface-accessible verdict. Surface expression is state-dependent: elevated on non-CBF AML LSCs, downregulated in HepG2 and on monocytes under fusogenic stimulation, and modulated by hypoxia in GBM. A pool of CD81 also resides in the ER/cytosol, inaccessible to extracellular binders.

surface_evidence.grade_rationale: CD81 surface accessibility is supported by multiple independent direct methods from multiple independent sources. (1) Live-cell flow cytometry (non-permeabilized) on HepG2, HEK293T, AML primary bone marrow blasts, and retinal organoid precursors across many studies confirms plasma membrane surface expression. (2) Surface biotinylation (membrane-impermeable Sulfo-NHS-SS-biotin) with streptavidin pulldown and LC-MS/MS or WB confirms surface-accessible CD81 in fibroblasts, glioma lines, and PBMC-adjacent protocols. (3) Immunoprecipitation from intact cells (original 1990 discovery) and immunogold EM on EVs provide additional orthogonal surface-detection evidence. No contradictory intracellular-only or conflicting evidence is present. The convergence of live-cell flow cytometry and surface biotinylation/MS from independent laboratories across diverse cell types and primary human samples firmly establishes direct_multi_method grade.

antibodies.name: anti-TAPA-1 (CD81) immunoprecipitation antibody
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Original discovery antibody; precipitated single 26 kDa chain from Triton X-100 lysates of intact cells.

expression_observations.context: Immunoprecipitation from intact human cell lines revealed 26 kDa TAPA-1 (CD81) as a cell-surface protein

antibodies.name: anti-TAPA-1 (CD81) antibody
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Used for antibody-induced down-modulation and co-modulation experiments with Leu-13; surface binding confirmed by reciprocal comodulation.

expression_observations.context: CD81 (TAPA-1) and Leu-13 surface expression on unspecified human cell lines; antibody-induced down-modulation confirmed surface co-localization

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 antibody (surface flow validation)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Wild-type HepG2 (CD81-negative) used as negative control; HepG2-hCD81 overexpression as positive reference.

expression_observations.context: Wild-type HepG2 cells: absent CD81 surface expression (grey histogram)
HepG2 cells stably overexpressing hCD81: high CD81 surface expression (red histogram)

antibodies.name: anti-CD19 antibody (surface staining)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: CD19-APEX2 surface delivery assessed; CD81 co-transfection enhances surface delivery 3-fold, confirming CD81 surface residency indirectly.

expression_observations.context: HEK293T cells co-transfected with CD19-APEX2 and CD81; 3-fold increase in CD19-APEX2 surface staining with CD81 co-expression, confirming CD81 surface residency and chaperone function

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 antibody (AML surface detection)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Validated by positive correlation of CD81 mRNA vs. surface protein abundance (r²=0.51) across n=47 AML samples and multiple independent cohorts.

expression_observations.context: AML bone marrow blasts at diagnosis vs. hematogones; representative histograms show differential CD81 surface expression (blasts green, hematogones purple)
Total AML study cohort (n=290) quantified by flow cytometry; CBF AML (n=26) vs. non-CBF AML (n=226); high CD81 surface expression associated with relapse and adverse outcome in non-CBF AML
CD81 mRNA correlated with cell-surface protein abundance by flow cytometry (r²=0.51, n=47 AML samples) validated across two independent cohorts

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 antibody (HEK293T surface panel)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Validated by single-cell CRISPR-Cas9 knockout of CD81 (and CD9, CD63); loss of signal in KO cells confirms antibody specificity.

expression_observations.context: HEK293T WT cells positive for CD9, CD63, and CD81 on cell surface; confirmed also on WT-EVs
CRISPR-Cas9 single-cell tetraspanin KO cells used as negative specificity controls for surface flow cytometry panel

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 antibody (immunogold EM)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Immunogold EM on intact EVs without permeabilization; nanometer-resolution distinguishes luminal from surface-exposed protein.

expression_observations.context: Immunogold EM visualization and quantification of CD9 and CD81 surface expression on EVs derived from cell lines

antibodies.name: MACSPlex Exosome Kit antibody panel (CD81 included)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: 37-epitope bead-based panel with two isotype control bead populations included for specificity assessment.

expression_observations.context: CD81 detected among 37 surface epitopes on MSC (CHo-MSC)-derived small extracellular vesicles by MACSPlex multiplex flow panel

Sulfo-NHS-SS-biotin surface biotinylation followed by MS (LC-MS/MS) on WI-38 lung fibroblasts; CD81 identified among 136 membrane/surface proteins out of 456 total, with ≥1 transmembrane domain confirmed
CD81 used as EV surface marker and loading control in western blot of HeLa whole-cell lysates, total EVs, and EV surface protein (biotinylated) preparations; enriched in surface fraction

Membrane-impermeable EZ-Link Sulfo-NHS-SS-biotin (1 mg/mL, pH 8, 30 min) labeling of glioma cell lines (U3034MG, U87MG, U3017MG, U3047MG) followed by glycine quench; surface-accessible proteins captured

CD81 identified by MS analysis among 9 proteins enriched by surface biotinylation and previously confirmed in proximity labeling; >2-fold enrichment confirmed by ≥2 independent biotinylation strategies (marked alongside CD9, ITGB1, etc.)

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 (SC-166029)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Used as EV marker alongside CD63, CD9, calnexin (intracellular ER negative control), and streptavidin-HRP for biotinylated proteins; 1:500 dilution.

antibodies.name: anti-Calnexin (intracellular negative control)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: ER marker used as negative control for surface/EV fractionation purity.

expression_observations.context: Western blot of EV-producing cells; CD81 detected as EV surface marker alongside calnexin (intracellular negative control); streptavidin-HRP detects biotinylated surface proteins

antibodies.name: rabbit anti-CD81 (ab219209)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Used at 1:200 dilution in IHC/IF panel on GBM tumor tissue. No genetic validation reported in this source.

expression_observations.context: IHC/IF on glioblastoma (GBM) tumor tissue sections; CD81 staining assessed alongside CD63, CD45, CD68, CD31, CD44, CD14, BCAN, TF, MCT2
Surface biotinylation of PBMCs (primary human immune cells) with LC-MS/MS surfaceome analysis; detailed in SI Appendix of GBM study
EV surface proteins labeled with EZ-Link Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin (10 mM, ThermoFisher Cat# 21331, PBS pH 7.4) after ultracentrifugation; surface-accessible EV proteins enriched for proteomics

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 antibody (retinal organoid sorting)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Validated by scRNA-seq identification of CD81 as photoreceptor-specific surface marker plus immunofluorescence confirmation.

expression_observations.context: Flow cytometry-based live-cell sorting of photoreceptor precursors from human retinal organoids using CD9-/CD81-mid surface marker strategy; validated by scRNA-seq and immunofluorescence
TAPA-1 (CD81) described as 26 kDa cell surface protein expressed on most human cell lines; foundational characterization from 1991 review/paper
Cryo-EM structural characterization of HCV sE1E2 heterodimer binding AR4A; informs surface-exposed CD81 LEL epitope engaged by HCV E2 during viral entry (recombinant/in vitro system)

non_surface_expression.context: Single-cell RNA sequencing of human retinal organoids used to identify photoreceptor-specific surface markers including CD81 (scRNA-seq component only; flow cytometry validation counted separately as direct surface evidence)
CD81 mRNA expression correlated with flow cytometry surface protein abundance in AML bone marrow blast cohorts (n=47 and independent validation cohorts); mRNA component of the correlation analysis

dual_localization.condition: during CD19 biosynthesis and trafficking in B lymphocytes

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Same cells with intracellular pool detected alongside plasma membrane pool
accessibility_modulation.change: HPA annotation places CD81 in both the plasma membrane and cytosol, indicating a split between surface and intracellular compartments.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Only the plasma membrane fraction is accessible to extracellular binders; the cytosolic pool is inaccessible without permeabilization, so surface-targeting agents reach a subset of total cellular CD81.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: B lymphocytes actively processing and trafficking CD19
accessibility_modulation.change: CD81 operates in both the ER (supporting CD19 glycosylation and ER exit) and at the plasma membrane (LEL association, TM2/TM3 signaling), demonstrating a pool in the ER alongside the surface pool.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: The ER-resident CD81 pool is inaccessible to surface-targeting binders; only the plasma membrane fraction can be engaged by antibodies or therapeutic agents without cell permeabilization.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Blood monocytes cultured under normal (non-fusogenic) in vitro conditions
accessibility_modulation.change: CD81 surface expression is upregulated when blood monocytes are placed in normal culture conditions, increasing surface CD81 and its integrin-complex formation.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Enhanced surface CD81 density upon in vitro culture increases the number of accessible epitopes per cell, improving the probability of productive engagement by surface-targeting binders on monocytes.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Human blood monocytes upregulating CD81 under normal culture conditions
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Human blood monocytes stimulated with Con A under fusogenic conditions
accessibility_modulation.change: Under Con A-driven fusogenic stimulation, the upregulation of CD81 surface expression seen in normal culture is inhibited, and CD81–integrin complex formation is down-regulated.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Fusogenic activation suppresses surface CD81 on monocytes, reducing the accessible target pool and potentially limiting efficacy of CD81-directed agents in this activated state.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Naive T cells versus memory T cells and Tregs (differential CD81-driven costimulation)
accessibility_modulation.change: CD81 surface engagement preferentially costimulates naive T cells over memory T-cell subsets and Tregs, implying differential functional accessibility or surface levels of CD81 across T-cell differentiation states.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Naive T cells are the primary functionally responsive subset; CD81-directed agents may differentially engage or activate naive versus memory/regulatory T cells depending on the T-cell state.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Normal bone marrow hematopoietic cells / normal AML blasts (CBF-AML, favorable prognosis)
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Non-CBF AML blasts (adverse prognosis) and leukemia stem cell (LSC)-enriched subpopulations at diagnosis
accessibility_modulation.change: Non-CBF AML blasts display higher surface CD81 than CBF-AML blasts; within AML, CD81 is further enriched on LSC-containing subpopulations where it supports stem-cell maintenance and chemotherapy resistance.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: High surface CD81 on aggressive AML blasts and LSCs makes these disease-state subpopulations more amenable to CD81-directed antibody or ADC targeting, with therapeutic relevance for relapse-prone patients.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Glioblastoma (GBM) cell lines under normoxic conditions
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: GBM cell lines under hypoxic stress conditions
accessibility_modulation.change: Hypoxic stress profoundly reshapes the GBM surface-endocytome; CD81 surface accessibility is modulated under hypoxia and validated as a potential ADC target candidate in this stress state.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Hypoxia-driven shifts in CD81 surface levels in GBM suggest that targeting efficiency of CD81-directed ADCs may differ between normoxic and hypoxic tumor regions, with hypoxic niches potentially presenting altered CD81 epitope density.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Normal hepatocytes (endogenous CD81 surface expression present)
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: HepG2 hepatocellular carcinoma cells (wild-type, no enforced overexpression)
accessibility_modulation.change: Wild-type HepG2 hepatocellular carcinoma cells lack endogenous surface CD81 expression, in contrast to normal hepatocytes that express surface CD81 required for Plasmodium entry.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Loss of surface CD81 in HepG2 cells means these tumor cells are inaccessible to CD81-directed surface agents unless CD81 is re-expressed; disease transformation can eliminate the target epitope.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Other retinal cell types in hiPSC-derived retinal organoids
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Photoreceptor precursor cells (CD9−CD81mid phenotype) within hiPSC-derived retinal organoids
accessibility_modulation.change: Photoreceptor precursors express an intermediate level of surface CD81 (CD81mid) distinguishable from other retinal cell types, allowing prospective FACS isolation at a specific developmental stage.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Intermediate surface CD81 on photoreceptor precursors provides a stage-specific surface handle for FACS sorting; the restricted CD81mid expression level means quantitative differences in epitope density relative to other retinal cells define targeting selectivity.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: B lymphocytes with intact CD81 (normal glycosylation and surface trafficking)
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: CD81-deficient B cells or B cells where CD81–CD19 glycosylation/trafficking is disrupted
accessibility_modulation.change: CD81 controls glycosylation and ER exit of CD19; loss of CD81 abolishes CD19 surface expression. CD81 itself requires intact N-terminal and TM domains for proper processing and surface delivery.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Proper glycosylation-dependent trafficking is required for CD81 to reach and remain at the plasma membrane; PTM or trafficking disruption eliminates the surface target, with direct implications for antibody or CAR-T accessibility on B cells.

structure.source: AlphaFold DB (STUB — fetchers not yet built)

co_receptor_requirements.rationale: CD81 chaperones CD19 through the ER and is required for CD19 surface delivery in B cells (loss of CD81 abolishes CD19 surface expression). CD81 itself reaches the plasma membrane independently across many non-B cell types, so CD19 is not required for CD81 surface delivery, but CD81 surface trafficking and complex formation can be modulated by partner availability.

restricted_subdomain.rationale: CD81 is associated with tetraspanin-enriched microdomains (lipid raft-associated), and cholesterol binding modulates its activity at the cell surface. This subdomain partitioning may limit access of large binders or alter epitope conformation, but does not preclude surface accessibility by antibodies, as confirmed by multiple live-cell binding assays.

ecd_size_assessment.rationale: CD81 has a 4-pass TM tetraspanin topology with two extracellular loops: a small extracellular loop (SEL, ~29 aa) and a large extracellular loop (LEL, ~89 aa, aa 113-201). The LEL is the primary antibody-binding and virus-docking domain. Total ECD is modest (~118 aa surfaced), placing this in the small-to-moderate class for binder engagement.

epitope_masking.rationale: The CD81 LEL is engaged by multiple partners (HCV E2, CD19, CD4/CD8, EWI proteins) within tetraspanin-enriched microdomains, and cholesterol binding modulates its conformation. Partner occlusion of the LEL surface could reduce accessible epitope area for antibody or ADC engagement depending on the cell type and activation state. Crystal and cryo-EM structures confirm LEL is surface-exposed but subject to conformational regulation.

evidence.claim: Immunoprecipitation from intact cells revealed a 26 kDa cell surface protein TAPA-1 (CD81), establishing cell-surface expression by antibody-based detection from the original discovery paper (1990). The antibody precipitated a single chain 26 kDa protein from Triton X-100 lysates, with additional associated proteins detected under milder CHAPS extraction, indicating surface-resident complex formation.

spans.quote: Immunoprecipitation studies revealed that the antibody reacted with a 26-kilodalton cell surface protein (TAPA-1).

evidence.claim: Direct measurement of cell-surface expression of TAPA-1 (CD81) and associated Leu-13 antigen by antibody binding and co-modulation experiments, confirming surface accessibility of both proteins and their association at the plasma membrane. Antibody-induced down-modulation of TAPA-1 surface expression was reciprocally coupled with Leu-13, confirming surface co-localization.

spans.quote: The cell-surface expression of both TAPA-1 (target of an antiproliferative antibody-1) and Leu-13 could be down-modulated by binding to their respective antibodies and they could be reciprocally comodulated.

evidence.claim: CD81 (TAPA-1) is described as a 26 kDa cell surface protein expressed on most human cell lines, establishing broad surface expression across human cell types. This foundational 1991 paper characterizes TAPA-1 as a surface protein detected by antiproliferative antibody.

spans.quote: TAPA-1 (the target of an antiproliferative antibody) is a 26-kDa cell surface protein expressed on most human cell lines.

evidence.claim: CD81 (TAPA-1) belongs to an evolutionarily conserved family of cell surface proteins all containing four transmembrane domains, establishing its tetraspanin 4TM topology. This structural classification defines CD81 as a 4-pass transmembrane protein with two extracellular loops.

spans.quote: TAPA-1 is a member of an evolutionarily related family of cell surface proteins all of which contain four transmembrane domains.

evidence.claim: Topology of CD81 (TAPA-1) was experimentally determined by proteolysis of in vitro translated protein embedded in microsomal membranes, providing direct biochemical evidence for the transmembrane orientation of the protein. This proteolysis-based topology assay is the primary experimental basis for the 4TM model.

cell_context.material_kind_other_label: in vitro translation / microsomal membrane system

spans.quote: A model is proposed for topology of TAPA-1 based on proteolysis studies in the in vitro translated protein embedded into microsomal membranes.

evidence.claim: Topology model for CD81 (TAPA-1) predicts cytoplasmic N- and C-termini with two hydrophilic extracellular regions, establishing the orientation of the large extracellular loop (LEL, aa 113-201) and the small extracellular loop (SEL, aa 34-63) as accessible from the cell surface. This is consistent with the UniProt 4TM annotation.

spans.quote: This analysis predicts that the amino and the carboxyl termini of the molecule are cytoplasmic and that the two hydrophilic regions of the molecule are extracellular.

evidence.claim: The antigenic epitope of CD81 (TAPA-1) recognized by the antiproliferative antibody is located within the second extracellular domain (the large extracellular loop, LEL). This localizes antibody binding to the surface-exposed LEL, confirming extracellular accessibility of this domain and its relevance as an antibody engagement site.

spans.quote: The antigenic epitope of the human TAPA-1 is contained within a subregion of the second extracellular domain of the protein.

evidence.claim: CD81 (TAPA-1) amino acid sequence analysis indicates four putative transmembrane domains and a potential N-myristoylation site, supporting the 4TM tetraspanin topology with intracellular N- and C-termini and two extracellular loops.

spans.quote: Analysis of the deduced amino acid sequence indicated that the protein is highly hydrophobic and that it contains four putative transmembrane domains and a potential N-myristoylation site.

evidence.claim: Crystal structure of the human CD81 large extracellular domain (CD81-LEL) was used for structural docking studies (Rosetta FastRelax protocols) to model HCV E2 peptide interactions with the extracellular face. Multiple PDB structures are available (1G8Q, 1IV5, 5DFV series, 5TCX, 6EJG), providing high-resolution structural evidence for the surface-exposed LEL conformation.

spans.quote: Because Ser 528 in epitope III is interchangeable with asparagine or threonine in the natural HCV variants, we tested Asn 528 - and Thr 528 -substituted peptides, assuming they take the same conformation displayed on the E2 structure, to see if they could dock onto the crystal structure of the human CD81 large extracellular domain (CD81-LEL) by using the Rosetta FastRelax protocols ( Fig. 5 ).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry on live HepG2 cells overexpressing human CD81 was used to validate and quantify CD81 surface expression; this is the primary validation method demonstrating that the overexpressed construct reaches the plasma membrane. The assay uses intact, non-permeabilized cells providing unambiguous surface localization evidence.

spans.quote: The resulting cells were validated by flow cytometry for surface expression of CD81 ( Figure 1a ).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry on live cells demonstrates that wild-type HepG2 cells lack CD81 surface expression (absent surface signal), while HepG2 cells stably overexpressing hCD81 show high surface CD81. This negative control vs. overexpression comparison provides clear evidence for plasma membrane CD81 surface accessibility in an overexpression system, with endogenous-negative cells as a specificity control.

spans.quote: As previously reported [ 12 ], CD81 surface expression is absent on wild-type HepG2 cells (grey histogram), whereas HepG2 cells overexpressing hCD81 show high CD81 surface expression (red histogram).

evidence.claim: Trafficking assay in HEK293T cells by flow cytometry demonstrates that CD81 co-expression promotes a 3-fold increase in CD19-APEX2 cell surface delivery, confirming that CD81 itself is surface-resident and capable of chaperoning CD19 to the plasma membrane. Flow cytometry on intact (non-permeabilized) cells measures surface staining specifically.

spans.quote: Properly folded CD19 is trafficked to the cell surface by forming a complex with the tetraspanin CD81. 29 As an initial test for the functional integrity of our fusion construct, we used a trafficking assay in HEK293T cells to assess the ability of CD81 to enhance the delivery of CD19-APEX2 to the cell surface. 30 , 31 Our CD19-APEX2 protein matches the response of wild-type CD19, showing a 3-fold increase in cell surface staining when co-transfected with CD81 ( Figures S1B and S1C ), confirming that the fusion protein was properly transported to the cell surface and that its interaction with

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry-based measurement of CD81 surface expression in AML patient bone marrow samples (retrospective cohorts) demonstrates clinically relevant surface CD81 in primary human AML blasts and leukemia stem cells (LSCs). High surface CD81 is associated with relapse and adverse clinical outcomes in non-CBF AML, establishing therapeutic relevance of the surface-accessible form.

cell_context.cell_type: acute myeloid leukemia blasts / leukemia stem cells

spans.quote: Analysis of retrospective patient cohorts revealed that high CD81 surface expression is associated with relapse and adverse clinical outcomes in non-core-binding factor AML.

evidence.claim: CD81 mRNA expression positively correlates with CD81 cell-surface protein abundance measured by flow cytometry (r²=0.51), validated across multiple independent AML cohorts (n=47). This correlation validates flow cytometry as the surface-detection method and shows mRNA can serve as a proxy for surface CD81 abundance in AML.

spans.quote: In order to independently validate this finding, we applied the positive correlation found in our cohort between CD81 mRNA and cell surface abundance determined by flow cytometry (FCM) (Fig. 1c ) on two independent cohorts of AML.

evidence.claim: Representative flow cytometry histograms of CD81 surface expression measured in AML bone marrow at diagnosis, comparing leukemic blasts (green) to hematogones (purple). This primary surface-detection assay uses live-cell antibody staining without permeabilization; the figure legend specifies the comparison populations and the measurement modality (flow cytometry histograms).

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: AML bone marrow blasts vs hematogones

spans.quote: High CD81 surface expression associates with relapse and adverse outcome in non-CBF AML. a Shown are representative flow cytometry histograms of CD81 surface expression measured in AML bone marrow taken at diagnosis with blasts (green) and corresponding hematogones (purple).

evidence.claim: Violin plots quantifying CD81 cell surface expression (by flow cytometry) across the total AML study cohort (n=290), stratified by CBF vs non-CBF subtypes (n=26, n=226). This large-cohort surface protein quantification provides strong statistical support for CD81 surface presence across AML patient samples.

cell_context.disease_state: acute myeloid leukemia (CBF and non-CBF subtypes)

spans.quote: The violin plot in the right panel summarizes low vs . high CD81 in the total AML study group (n = 290). b Violin plot comparing CD81 cell surface expression in CBF vs . non-CBF AML (n = 26, n = 226) and c scatterplot correlating CD81 z-score normalized mRNA vs .

evidence.claim: Flow cytometric analysis of intact HEK293T wild-type cells confirms surface expression of CD81 (along with CD9 and CD63) on both cells and their extracellular vesicles (WT-EVs). This provides surface-expression evidence for CD81 in a commonly used human cell line without permeabilization, confirming plasma membrane localization.

spans.quote: Flow cytometric analysis showed that HEK293T WT cells and WT‐EVs were positive for CD9, CD63, and CD81 on their surface (Figure 2B,C ).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry used to validate tetraspanin (CD9, CD63, CD81) surface expression on cells and EVs following single-cell CRISPR-Cas9 knockout of individual tetraspanins. The KO panel provides genetic specificity controls for the surface detection antibody assay, confirming antibody specificity for the CD81 surface epitope.

spans.quote: Flow cytometry validation of tetraspanins surface expression on the cell and the EVs after single‐cell knockout of tetraspanins.

evidence.claim: Immunogold electron microscopy (iEM) used to directly visualize and quantify surface expression of CD81 (and CD9) on extracellular vesicles derived from cell lines. Immunogold EM provides nanometer-resolution confirmation of CD81 at the membrane surface of EVs, without permeabilization, distinguishing luminal from surface-exposed protein.

spans.quote: To further characterize the EVs derived from cell lines, we performed immunogold EM (iEM) to visualize and quantify the surface expression of CD9 and CD81.

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry panel using MACSPlex Exosome Kit (Miltenyi Biotec) simultaneously detects 37 surface epitopes including CD81 on small extracellular vesicles (sEVs), with two isotype control bead populations included in the panel. This methodological approach provides multi-epitope surface detection with isotype controls for specificity validation.

spans.quote: Isolated sEVs were characterized by flow cytometry using the MACSPlex Exosome Kit (Miltenyi Biotec, Bergisch Gladbach, Germany), which was developed for the simultaneous detection of 37 surface epitopes (CD1c, CD2, CD3, CD4, CD8, CD9, CD11c, CD14, CD19, CD20, CD24, CD25, CD29, CD31, CD40, CD41b, CD42a, CD44, CD45, CD49e, CD56, CD62P, CD63, CD69, CD81, CD86, CD105, CD133, CD142, CD146, CD209, CD326, HLA-ABC, HLA-DRDPDQ, MCSP, ROR1 and SSEA-4) that are known to be present on different exosomes and two isotype control beads.

evidence.claim: Surfaceome MS analysis (LC-MS/MS) identified CD81 among 456 proteins detected, with UniProt categorizing it as having transmembrane domains (65 proteins including CD81, CD9, and CD63 have at least 1 and up to 12 TM domains). This confirms CD81 topology as a multi-pass TM protein in a mass spectrometry surfaceome dataset.

spans.quote: Uniprot categorized 136 of the 456 proteins (29.82%) proteins as being present in membranes, on the cell membrane, or on the cell surface; 65 of them, including CD81, CD9, and CD63, have at least one (and up to 12) transmembrane domains ( Dataset S15 ).

evidence.claim: Surface protein isolation by sulfo-NHS-SS-biotin surface biotinylation followed by streptavidin pulldown and mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) and western blot analysis was used to characterize surface proteins on both cells and extracellular vesicles from WI-38 fibroblasts. This method family (surface_biotinylation + mass_spec_surfaceome) enables identification of surface-accessible proteins without permeabilization.

spans.quote: Surface proteins on cells and EVs were isolated for mass spectrometry (MS) and western blot analysis.

evidence.claim: CD81 was used as an extracellular vesicle marker and loading control in western blot analysis of HeLa whole-cell lysates, total EVs, and EV surface protein preparations. This WB usage as an EV surface marker validates that CD81 is enriched in the surface protein fraction (biotinylated) of EVs, corroborating its surface accessibility.

spans.quote: We first validated the production of DPP4-Myc fusion protein, along with loading control GAPDH and EV marker CD81, in HeLa whole-cell lysates, total EVs, and EV surface protein preparations.

evidence.claim: Surface biotinylation protocol using membrane-impermeable EZ-Link Sulfo-NHS-SS-biotin (1 mg/mL, 30 min) at pH 8 with MgCl2/CaCl2-PBS washes, followed by glycine quench (0.1 M, 10 min) to stop the reaction before surface protein processing. This method specifically labels surface-accessible lysines on intact cells without cell permeabilization.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: glioma cell lines (U3034MG, U87MG, U3017MG, U3047MG)

spans.quote: Cells were washed with PBS (with MgCl 2 and CaCl 2 Mg/Ca-PBS) at pH 8 prior to incubation with a 1 mg/mL solution of membrane-impermeable biotin (EZ-Link Sulfo-N-hydroxysuccinimide-SS-biotin, Thermo Scientific, 21331) for 30 min, rinsed, incubated with 0.1 M glycine for 10 min to stop the reaction, and rinsed again prior to processing of surface biotinylated proteins (surfaceome).

evidence.claim: Surface biotinylation followed by MS analysis identified CD81 (CD81 antigen) as one of nine proteins enriched by surface biotinylation and previously confirmed in proximity labeling experiments, providing multi-method convergent evidence for CD81 cell-surface localization. CD81 was among the most consistently surface-detected proteins alongside CD9, ITGB1, and other known surface proteins.

spans.quote: MS analysis identified GCPII as the most enriched protein, along with eight additional proteins previously detected in proximity labeling experiments: CD81 (CD81 antigen), CD9 (CD9 antigen), GPM6A (neuronal membrane glycoprotein M6-a), ITGB1 (integrin β1), MYADM (myeloid-associated differentiation marker), PROCR (endothelial protein C receptor), PTGFRN (prostaglandin F2 receptor negative regulator), and THY1 (Thy-1 membrane glycoprotein) (Fig. 3 B).

evidence.claim: CD81 is among eight proteins showing >2-fold enrichment confirmed by at least two independent biotinylation strategies, providing high-confidence multi-method validation of cell-surface localization. The requirement for concordance across multiple biotinylation approaches substantially reduces false-positive surface assignment risk.

spans.quote: Eight proteins exhibiting > 2-fold enrichment and identified previously by at least two biotinylation strategies are marked in blue (CD81, CD9, GPM6A, ITGB1, MYADM, PROCR, PTGFRN, and THY1).

evidence.claim: Surface biotinylation followed by streptavidin bead enrichment and western blot analysis was used to detect and validate surface-accessible proteins. This paired WB + fractionation (surface biotinylation → streptavidin pulldown → WB) step provides the required paired western blot confirmation for the surface biotinylation MS result.

spans.quote: Following cell lysis and protein enrichment on streptavidin beads, we performed Western blot analysis of the resulting enriched proteins.

evidence.claim: Western blot antibody details: anti-CD81 clone SC-166029 (Santa Cruz, 1:500) used as EV marker alongside CD63, CD9, calnexin, and streptavidin-HRP. Calnexin serves as an intracellular (ER) negative control for surface/EV fractionation, and streptavidin-HRP detects biotinylated proteins. This panel constitutes a validated positive/negative control set for EV surface protein western blotting.

spans.quote: Membranes were then incubated with the following primary antibodies overnight at 4°C: CD63 (1:1000, EMD Millipore #CBL553), CD81 (1:500, Santa Cruz #SC‐166029), CD9 (1:500, Cell Signaling #D801A), Calnexin (1:1000, Cell signaling #2433S), Annexin 1 (1:500, Santa Cruz #SC‐12740), HSP90B1 (1:1000, Cell Signaling #7411), and Streptavidin HRP (1:10,000, Cell Signaling #3999) all diluted in 5% BSA.

evidence.claim: Anti-CD81 antibody details: rabbit anti-CD81 (Abcam ab219209, 1:200 dilution) used in IHC/IF panel. This antibody catalog reference anchors the AntibodyRef for surface detection assays in the GBM tumor microenvironment study.

spans.quote: Thermo Fisher Scientific, PA5-75459), mouse anti-CD63 (1/100, Abcam, ab8219), mouse anti-CD45-PE (1/25, BD Biosciences, 555483), rabbit anti-CD68 (1/800, Cell Signaling Technology, D4B9C), mouse anti-CD31 (1/100, Dako, JC70A), mouse anti-CD44 (1/100, Dako, DF1485), mouse anti-CD14-PE (1/25, BioLegend), rabbit anti-BCAN (1/200, Abcam, ab111719), rabbit anti-CD81 (1/200, Abcam, ab219209), rabbit anti-TF (kindly provided by Wolfram Ruf, Center for Thrombosis and Hemostasis, University of Mainz, Mainz, Germany), or rabbit anti-MCT2 (1/100, Thermo Fisher Scientific, PA5-112712).

evidence.claim: Surface biotinylation of PBMCs (peripheral blood mononuclear cells) is referenced in the SI Appendix of this study, establishing that a cell-surface biotinylation protocol was applied to primary human immune cells alongside LC-MS/MS surfaceome analysis. This anchors method_family=surface_biotinylation for primary PBMC samples.

spans.quote: Detailed descriptions of the mouse GBM model, Western blotting, ligand uptake, biotinylation of PBMCs and CD14 + magnetic cell separation sorting, Incucyte cytotox assay, and GSEA and pathway analysis of LC-MS/MS data are listed in SI Appendix .

evidence.claim: Surface biotinylation of extracellular vesicles (mEVs and sEVs) using EZ-Link Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin (10 mM, ThermoFisher Cat# 21331) in PBS pH 7.4 after ultracentrifugation pellet resuspension. This membrane-impermeable reagent labels surface-accessible amines on intact EV membranes, providing methodological detail for MethodObservation with method_subclass=EV surface labeling.

spans.quote: Following the first ultracentrifugation steps for mEVs and sEVs at 16,500 g and 100,000 g, respectively, pellets were resuspended in 10 mM EZ-Link™ Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin (Cat No. 21331, ThermoFisher Scientific, Mississauga, ON, Canada) in PBS, pH 7.4.

evidence.claim: Surface-accessible proteins on EVs were enriched by Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin labeling followed by proteomics analysis, used in combination with global proteomic analysis. This method specifically captures surface-exposed EV proteins including CD81 as a canonical EV surface marker, without permeabilization of the EV membrane.

spans.quote: Proteomics samples were then analyzed using two approaches based on (1) global proteomic analysis and (2) enrichment of EV surface proteins by labeling surface-accessible proteins with a Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin reagent.

evidence.claim: HCV E1E2 ectodomains were scaffolded using coiled coils to produce soluble heterodimers (sE1E2) that bind the broadly neutralizing antibody AR4A and were structurally resolved by cryo-EM. This is directly relevant to CD81 as HCV E2 binds the CD81 LEL as its receptor-binding domain; the cryo-EM structure informs the surface-exposed epitope of CD81 LEL engaged by the virus during HCV entry.

spans.quote: Recently, heterodimeric coiled coils were used to scaffold HCV E1E2 ectodomains, resulting in measurable AR4A binding 77 and enabling the first cryo-EM structure of an sE1E2 heterodimer 71 .

evidence.claim: Single-cell RNA sequencing of human retinal organoids identified photoreceptor-specific surface markers including CD81 (as part of CD9-/CD81-mid sorting strategy), validated by immunofluorescence and flow cytometry. The combined scRNA-seq + flow cytometry validation confirms surface accessibility of CD81 on photoreceptor precursors for cell sorting applications.

spans.quote:
Surface marker-based sorting offers a safer alternative.

Methods

We analyzed single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) datasets of human retinal organoids to identify PR-specific surface markers and validated them by immunofluorescence and flow cytometry.

evidence.claim: HPA database annotation places CD81 in both the plasma membrane and cytosol compartments, supporting dual surface and intracellular localization.

spans.quote: HPA subcellular locations: Plasma membrane;Cytosol.

evidence.claim: CD81 is expressed broadly across hematopoietic cell types including B cells, T cells, monocytes, and dendritic cells in human blood.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: B cells, T cells, monocytes, dendritic cells

spans.quote: CD 81 is expressed by many cells of the hematopoietic system, including B cells, T cells, monocytes, and dendritic cells.

evidence.claim: CD81 is expressed in multiple myeloma (MM) bone marrow microenvironment with broad impact on immune cell infiltration, particularly monocytes and M2 macrophages.

spans.quote: In our previous study, we successfully constructed a prognostic model for MM based on inflammatory response-related genes (IRRGs); furthermore, we demonstrated that one of the IRRGs, CD81, has a broad impact on the infiltration of immune cells, particularly monocytes and M2 macrophages [ 3 ].

evidence.claim: In multiple myeloma, high CD81 expression in bone marrow correlates with decreased peripheral CD8+ T cell counts and increased M2 macrophage (CD206+) infiltration, indicating disease-state-dependent immune cell distribution modulated by CD81.

spans.quote: High CD81 expression correlated with decreased peripheral CD8 + T cell counts and increased M2 macrophage infiltration (CD206+), both linked to poorer outcomes.

evidence.claim: CD81 is expressed on the surface of human T cells where it physically associates with CD4 and CD8 coreceptors, indicating surface presence on both CD4+ and CD8+ T cell subsets.

spans.quote: Cluster of differentiation 81 (CD81) is a widely expressed tetraspanin molecule that physically associates with CD4 and CD8 on the surface of human T cells.

evidence.claim: CD81 surface engagement preferentially costimulates primary human naïve T cells over memory T-cell subsets and Tregs, demonstrating cell-state-dependent differential surface CD81 functional accessibility across T-cell subsets.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: naïve T cells, memory T cells, Tregs

cell_context.activation_state: naïve vs memory vs Treg subsets

spans.quote: Here we show that CD81 and CD28 have a preference for different subsets of T cells: Primary human naïve T cells are better costimulated by CD81, whereas the memory T-cell subsets and Tregs are better costimulated by CD28.

evidence.claim: CD81 surface expression is upregulated when blood monocytes are cultured under normal (non-fusogenic) conditions, demonstrating activation-state-dependent surface modulation in monocytes.

spans.quote: Expression of CD9 and CD81 and their complex formation with integrins were up-regulated when blood monocytes were cultured under normal conditions.

evidence.claim: Under fusogenic conditions (Con A), upregulation of CD81 surface expression on monocytes is inhibited, demonstrating downward modulation of surface CD81 in a fusogenic/activation state.

spans.quote: Under fusogenic conditions in the presence of Con A, CD9 and CD81 up-regulation was inhibited, and their complex formation with integrins was down-regulated.

evidence.claim: CD81 is expressed on the surface of human monocytes and alveolar macrophages; anti-CD81 antibodies promote fusion of these cells, confirming surface accessibility in both cell types.

spans.quote: Anti-CD9 and -CD81 antibodies, which were previously shown to inhibit the fusion of gametes, myoblasts, and virus-infected cells, unexpectedly promoted the fusion of monocytes and alveolar macrophages.

evidence.claim: In CD9/CD81-null mice, alveolar macrophages (lung) and bone marrow cells form larger numbers of multinucleated cells than wild-type, demonstrating CD81 expression and functional role in alveolar macrophages and bone marrow cells.

spans.quote: When stimulated in vitro or in vivo, alveolar macrophages and bone marrow cells of CD9- and CD81-null mice formed larger numbers of multinucleated cells than those of wild-type mice.

evidence.claim: CD9/CD81 double-null mice spontaneously develop multinucleated giant cells in the lung and show enhanced osteoclastogenesis in bone, establishing CD81 functional expression in lung macrophages and bone tissue.

spans.quote: Finally, CD9/CD81 double-null mice spontaneously developed multinucleated giant cells in the lung and showed enhanced osteoclastogenesis in the bone.

evidence.claim: A human patient with homozygous CD81 loss-of-function mutation shows complete absence of CD81 expression on blood leukocytes, demonstrating that CD81 is normally present on circulating leukocytes but requires intact CD81 gene.

spans.quote: The patient had normal CD19 alleles but carried a homozygous CD81 mutation resulting in a complete lack of CD81 expression on blood leukocytes.

evidence.claim: In CD81-deficient patient B cells, CD19 membrane expression is critically dependent on CD81, revealing that CD81 controls surface localization of a key B cell co-receptor; absence of CD81 disrupts CD19 surface display.

spans.quote: Retroviral transduction and glycosylation experiments on EBV-transformed B cells from the patient revealed that CD19 membrane expression critically depended on CD81.

evidence.claim: CD81-deficient human B cells show impaired activation upon BCR stimulation but T cell subsets and functions are unaffected, indicating CD81 is functionally required on B cells but not T cells for antigen-receptor signaling.

spans.quote: Similar to CD19-deficient patients, CD81-deficient patients had B cells that showed impaired activation upon stimulation via the B cell antigen receptor but no overt T cell subset or function defects.

evidence.claim: CD81 plays multiple roles in intracellular trafficking and plasma membrane functions of CD19 in B lymphocytes, operating in both ER and plasma membrane compartments.

spans.quote: We found that CD81 plays multiple roles in the processing, intracellular trafficking, and membrane functions of CD19.

evidence.claim: CD81 domain mapping in B lymphocytes reveals distinct roles in the ER (glycosylation of CD19, ER exit support) and at the plasma membrane (LEL association with CD19, TM2/TM3-mediated signaling), confirming CD81 occupies both ER and plasma membrane compartments in B cells.

spans.quote: Surprisingly, these different roles are embodied in distinct CD81 domains, which function in the different cellular compartments: the N-terminal tail of CD81 has an effect on the glycosylation of CD19; the first transmembrane domain of CD81 is sufficient to support the exit of CD19 from the endoplasmic reticulum, although the large extracellular loop (LEL) of CD81 associates physically with CD19 early during biosynthesis; and finally, the TM2 and TM3 domains of CD81 play a role in the transmission of signals initiated upon engagement of the LEL.

evidence.claim: CD81 is expressed on hepatocytes and is required for Plasmodium falciparum (human) and P. yoelii (rodent) sporozoite infectivity, establishing hepatocyte surface CD81 as a functional entry receptor in human liver.

spans.quote: Here we show that the tetraspanin CD81, a putative receptor for hepatitis C virus, is required on hepatocytes for human Plasmodium falciparum and rodent Plasmodium yoelii sporozoite infectivity.

evidence.claim: CD81-deficient mouse hepatocytes fail to support Plasmodium sporozoite infection in vivo and in vitro; anti-human CD81 antibodies block P. falciparum hepatic development, confirming that CD81 is surface-accessible on hepatocytes and required for parasite entry.

spans.quote: P. yoelii sporozoites fail to infect CD81-deficient mouse hepatocytes, in vivo and in vitro, and antibodies against mouse and human CD81 inhibit in vitro the hepatic development of P. yoelii and P. falciparum, respectively.

evidence.claim: CD81 surface expression is absent on wild-type HepG2 hepatocellular carcinoma cells but present at high levels when hCD81 is overexpressed, establishing that HepG2 cells lack endogenous surface CD81 and serving as a controlled model for surface CD81 function studies.

spans.quote: As previously reported [ 12 ], CD81 surface expression is absent on wild-type HepG2 cells (grey histogram), whereas HepG2 cells overexpressing hCD81 show high CD81 surface expression (red histogram).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry shows CD81 surface expression on AML bone marrow blasts at diagnosis, with a bimodal distribution across patient blasts; corresponding hematogones (normal B-cell precursors) also express CD81 at the surface, allowing diagnostic gating.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: AML bone marrow blasts and hematogones

spans.quote: Fig. 1 High CD81 surface expression associates with relapse and adverse outcome in non-CBF AML. a Shown are representative flow cytometry histograms of CD81 surface expression measured in AML bone marrow taken at diagnosis with blasts (green) and corresponding hematogones (purple).

evidence.claim: A bimodal distribution of CD81 surface expression exists on AML blasts: CD81-Low and CD81-High subpopulations, representing distinct cell states with different surface CD81 levels in AML bone marrow.

spans.quote: Similarly, we detected a bimodal distribution of CD81 surface expression on blasts in this larger cohort (Supplementary Methods and Fig. 2 ; Fig. 1a ).

evidence.claim: CBF-AML (favorable prognosis) shows lower CD81 cell surface expression compared to non-CBF AML (poor prognosis), demonstrating disease-subtype-dependent modulation of surface CD81 levels in AML bone marrow blasts.

spans.quote: In this study, we show that core-binding factor (CBF) AML (n = 26), which have well-known favorable prognosis, 3 have lower CD81 cell surface expression compared to non-CBF AML (Fig. 1b , Supplementary Fig. 3a ).

evidence.claim: CD81 surface expression is enriched within leukemia stem cell (LSC)-containing subpopulations in AML bone marrow, where it supports LSC maintenance and chemotherapy resistance — indicating that CD81 surface levels are highest in stem-like, therapy-resistant AML cell states.

spans.quote: Notably, CD81 was enriched within LSC-containing subpopulations, where its expression supported LSC maintenance and resistance to chemotherapy.

evidence.claim: In hiPSC-derived retinal organoids, photoreceptor precursors can be identified and isolated by a CD9-negative/CD81-intermediate (CD9⁻CD81mid) surface phenotype via FACS, establishing that CD81 is expressed at the surface of retinal photoreceptor precursor cells at intermediate levels.

spans.quote: Cells with a CD9⁻CD81<sup>mid</sup> (negative for CD9 and intermediate levels of CD81) phenotype were isolated via fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) and transplanted into the subretinal space of RCS rats.

evidence.claim: The CD9⁻CD81mid surface phenotype effectively enriches photoreceptor precursors with high purity and low proliferative activity from hiPSC-derived retinal organoids; transplanted cells survive, integrate, and differentiate into rods/cones, validating CD81 as a surface marker distinguishing photoreceptor precursor from other retinal cell states.

cell_context.cell_type: photoreceptor precursors vs other retinal cell types

spans.quote:
Post-transplantation survival, integration, differentiation, and therapeutic efficacy were assessed through immunofluorescence, quantification of outer nuclear layer (ONL) thickness, scotopic flash electroretinography (fERG) and light/dark box test.

Results

Among the identified markers (STX3, PARD6B, PROM1, CD9⁻CD81mid), the CD9⁻CD81mid phenotype effectively enriched PR precursors with high purity and low proliferative activity.

evidence.claim: CD81 shows cytoplasmic and membranous expression in germ cells within seminiferous tubules of testis tissue, as confirmed by IHC in a study using retinoblastoma tissue; testis germ cells serve as positive control for CD81 expression.

spans.quote: CD81: Cytoplasmic and membranous expression of the germ cells within the seminiferous tubules was considered as positive control ( Fig. 1 c1).

evidence.claim: CD81 IHC analysis was performed on ovarian cancer (OvCa) tissue sections, detecting CD81 as an exosomal surface marker in the tumor microenvironment, reflecting expression in the ovarian cancer tissue context.

spans.quote: We have performed IHC analysis for exosomal surface marker CD81 in OvCa tissues as exosomes participate in preparing a premetastatic niche (PMN) for the migrating cancer cells at the secondary site and promote metastasis [ 32 ].

evidence.claim: In glioblastoma (GBM) cell lines, hypoxic stress profoundly reshapes the surface-endocytome landscape; CD81 is identified as a validated potential ADC target candidate whose surface accessibility is modulated by hypoxic conditions.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: GBM cell lines (U3034MG, U87MG, U3017MG, U3047MG)

spans.quote: We reveal how hypoxic stress profoundly reshapes the GBM surface-endocytome, and validate several potential ADC target candidates; CXADR, CD47, CD81, BSG, and FXYD6.

evidence.claim: Cholesterol binding modulates CD81 activity at the cell surface, suggesting a lipid-dependent mechanism for regulation of CD81 surface function across cellular contexts.

spans.quote: Cholesterol binding appears to modulate CD81 activity in cells, suggesting a potential mechanism for regulation of tetraspanin function.

confidence_reasoning: A1 evidence grade is direct_multi_method with zero contradicting evidence entries; multiple independent laboratories using live-cell flow cytometry (CRISPR-KO validated), surface biotinylation/LC-MS/MS, and immunogold EM converge on the same surface-accessible verdict across primary human cells, cell lines, and organoids. A2 adds moderate state-dependence (AML disease state, monocyte activation, HepG2 tumor transformation, hypoxia) and confirms dual plasma-membrane/ER/cytosol localization, but none of these constitute a dominant intracellular or secreted form that would downgrade the grade. The integrated verdict therefore tracks A1's direct_multi_method grade at high confidence.
